policy_statement: By 2030, establish a shared and co-managed vehicle fleet across City Council, CPAS and para-communal non-profits, and reduce/optimize fleet use (20% fleet reduction vs 2018; share 70% of non-utility vehicles internally; 100% low-carbon engines where alternatives exist).
mechanism_description: The City Council, CPAS and para-communal non-profits will pool and optimize vehicle use through a shared fleet and shift cars to low-carbon engines where alternatives exist, reducing emissions from fleet travel.
primary_causal_pathway: Fleet operations change (↓ vehicle-km + ↓ fuel carbon intensity) → ↓ transport fuel combustion/energy use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Public/para-public organizations consolidate and co-manage fleets, increase vehicle sharing, right-size the fleet, and replace ICE vehicles with low-carbon alternatives (e.g., EVs, hydrogen, sustainable fuels) where feasible.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced vehicle-kilometers and improved utilization lower total energy demand; lower-carbon powertrains/fuels reduce CO₂ (and some co-pollutant) emissions per kilometer; upstream electricity/fuel supply emissions may also decrease depending on grid/fuel pathway.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle transport-sector GHG emissions from municipal/organizational fleet travel.
dominant_pathway_test: Optimizing and electrifying/low-carbonizing a vehicle fleet reduces fuel combustion per km and total km traveled, producing the largest climate-relevant effect: lower transport GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Changes to fleet size/utilization and vehicle technology reduce fossil fuel use and carbon intensity, leading to lower GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While fleet optimization is also an efficiency measure, the stated and dominant climate outcome across all such policies is emissions reduction from transport energy/fuel use; this is more directly mitigation than generic resource efficiency framing.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “fleet optimization” policy were solely about cost or service reliability with no material change in fuel/energy use, classification could weaken—but this canonical mechanism explicitly includes low-carbon transition and reduced use, anchoring mitigation.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism (optimize/shared fleet + shift to low-carbon engines/fuels) has its biggest climate effect through reduced transport GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Details like whether reductions come mainly from VKT reduction vs electrification, and lifecycle vs tailpipe accounting, require policy-specific evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “low-carbon engines” include modest-efficiency ICE upgrades without fuel switching, emissions benefits may be smaller; if electrification occurs on a high-carbon grid, net lifecycle gains depend on electricity mix—still primarily mitigation, but magnitude varies.
key_indicators: "By 2030"; "shared and co-managed fleet"; "reducing its fleet ... by 20% compared with 2018"; "sharing 70% of non-utility vehicles internally"; "100% of the car fleet uses a low-carbon engine"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants (NOx/PM), lower operating costs, reduced congestion/parking demand, improved asset utilization
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an implicit operational/planning component (streamline/pool vehicles) and an implicit procurement shift (low-carbon engines), but the presence of clear quantified goals makes “target-setting” the dominant instrument type.
